title: The primitive types — string, number, boolean
instruction:
Every value in TypeScript has a type. The three most fundamental are `string`, `number`, and `boolean`. TypeScript infers them automatically from literals.

Declare variables and see TypeScript's inferred types.
successMessage: Three types cover most variables. TypeScript uses one `number` type for all numeric values — no `int` vs `float`. `2850` and `9.99` are both `number`.

title: null and undefined — the billion-dollar mistake
instruction:
Tony Hoare called null "my billion-dollar mistake". TypeScript's strict mode separates `null` and `undefined` from other types — you must explicitly opt in to allow them.

See the difference between null and undefined in TypeScript.
successMessage: In TypeScript strict mode, `number` does NOT include `null` or `undefined` — you must write `number | null` explicitly. This eliminates most "undefined is not a function" errors.

title: any — the escape hatch (use sparingly)
instruction:
`any` opts out of type checking entirely. It's useful when migrating JavaScript code or working with truly dynamic data — but every `any` is a potential runtime error waiting to happen.

See how `any` disables type checking.
successMessage: `any` is useful during migrations but dangerous in production. Prefer `unknown` — it requires narrowing before use, maintaining safety while still accepting any value.

title: Template literal types — string patterns
instruction:
Template literal types create new string types by combining string literals. They're used for route patterns, event names, and any string that follows a specific format.

Build typed event names using template literal types.
successMessage: `EventName` is the union of all 9 combinations. TypeScript prevents you from registering `"payment:failed"` — it's not in the allowed set. This is a zero-cost way to prevent typos in event names.

title: Literal types — exact values
instruction:
A literal type is a type that allows only one specific value. Combined with union types, literal types create powerful, exhaustive enumerations without the overhead of enums.

Build a status machine with literal types.
successMessage: `Record<OrderStatus, string>` is exhaustive — TypeScript errors if any status is missing from the `colors` map. Adding a new status to the union immediately highlights every place that needs updating.

title: bigint — large integers
instruction:
`bigint` handles integers larger than `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` (2^53 - 1). It's used in cryptography, database IDs, and financial calculations that need exact precision.

Calculate with large numbers using bigint.
successMessage: `number` loses precision above 2^53 — `maxSafe + 1` and `maxSafe + 2` are the same! `bigint` maintains exact precision. Use `n` suffix for bigint literals: `1n`, `42n`, `9007199254740993n`.

title: symbol — unique identifiers
instruction:
Every `Symbol()` call creates a unique, non-comparable value. Symbols are used as unique property keys that can't accidentally collide with other keys.

Use symbols as unique identifiers.
successMessage: Symbols are unique even with the same description. Symbol-keyed properties are invisible to `Object.keys()`, `JSON.stringify()`, and `for...in` loops — they're private by convention.

title: Type widening — const vs let
instruction:
TypeScript widens types for `let` (reassignable) but keeps them narrow for `const` (fixed). This affects how types flow through your code.

Observe the difference between const and let inference.
successMessage: `const` creates a literal type (`"hello"`) while `let` creates a wide type (`string`). `as const` forces a literal type on a `let` variable. In real code, `as const` is rarely needed — use `const` instead.

title: Type assertions — when you know better
instruction:
Sometimes TypeScript can't figure out the type from context, but you know. Type assertions (`as Type`) tell TypeScript to trust you — but they don't perform runtime conversion.

Assert types when working with DOM elements and JSON.
successMessage: Type assertions bypass type checking — you're telling TypeScript "trust me, I know the shape". Use them when you know more than TypeScript (e.g., API responses). Prefer type guards over assertions when possible — they're checked at runtime.

title: Non-null assertion — telling TypeScript a value exists
instruction:
The non-null assertion operator `!` tells TypeScript a value is definitely not null or undefined. It's a compile-time-only assertion — no runtime check happens.

Use non-null assertions when you're certain a value exists.
successMessage: `!` removes null/undefined from the type — use it only when you're certain the value exists. Prefer `?.` (optional chaining) + `??` (nullish coalescing) — they're checked at runtime and won't crash if the value is null.

title: Checking types at runtime — typeof and instanceof
instruction:
`typeof` checks primitive types at runtime. `instanceof` checks if a value is an instance of a class. TypeScript narrows the type based on these checks.

Write a function that handles multiple types.
successMessage: Inside each `case`, TypeScript narrows the type. In `case "string"`, TypeScript knows `value` is `string` — so `.toUpperCase()` is available. In `case "number"`, `.toFixed()` is available.

title: Type predicates — custom narrowing functions
instruction:
A type predicate `value is Type` in a function return type tells TypeScript to narrow the type when the function returns `true`. This is how you create reusable narrowing logic.

Write type predicates for API response validation.
successMessage: After `if (isSuccess(r))`, TypeScript narrows `r` to `SuccessResponse` — `.data` is accessible. In the `else` branch, it's `ErrorResponse` — `.code` and `.message` are accessible. Clean, reusable narrowing.

title: Satisfies vs as — validation without widening
instruction:
`as` asserts a type (bypasses checking). `satisfies` validates against a type but preserves the inferred type. Both are useful, but `satisfies` is safer.

Compare the two approaches for a route configuration.
successMessage: `satisfies` validates that every element matches `Route` (e.g., method must be one of the four HTTP methods) while keeping each element's inferred type. `as Route[]` would also work but provides less type safety.

title: Branded types — prevent value mix-ups
instruction:
Branded types prevent mixing up values of the same underlying type. A `UserId` and an `OrderId` are both `number`, but you should never accidentally pass one where the other is expected.

Create branded types for IDs.
successMessage: Branded types add a phantom property that exists only in TypeScript's type system — no runtime overhead. `UserId` and `OrderId` are both `number` at runtime, but TypeScript prevents them from being swapped. This catches a whole class of bugs.
